eyebrow: Biome Palette · Sourced from real ecosystems
tagline: Forty-eight biomes, read for their colour, not their postcard.
metaNote:
Color sourced from flora,
mineral, water & seasonal light
sourcing: Every palette is sampled from a specific ecosystem at a specific season and hour — its dominant flora, its mineral ground, the colour of its water, and the quality of its light. The aim is the colour you would actually see standing there, not the saturated idea of 'nature' that stock imagery sells.

volumes.title: Alpine & High Mountain
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol I · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from above the treeline, each in its working season.
preface: High mountains share a grammar — bleached rock, wind-pruned scrub, glacial water that is never the blue of a swimming pool — but the species and stone change with the hemisphere. These four are sampled at the elevation where forest gives out.
Each refuses the travel-poster snowcap: the colour that actually fills the eye at altitude is rock, lichen, and dead grass, not white.

palettes.kicker: 36° N · October · 11:00 · Hotaka range above Kamikōchi, Japanese Northern Alps
palettes.title: The cirque at mid-morning, larch turning gold against granite while the haimatsu pine stays black-green
palettes.source: Color from the Japanese Alps in kōyō season: karamatsu larch needles going gold, dwarf haimatsu creeping pine, pale granite scree, the glacial milk of the Azusa, and the first rime on the north faces.

swatches.name: Granite scree, pale grey
swatches.note: Weathered alpine granite that fills the cirque floor and walls.

swatches.name: Karamatsu larch, autumn gold
swatches.note: Japanese larch needles at peak turn, the loudest colour of the season.

swatches.name: Haimatsu pine, black-green
swatches.note: Dwarf creeping pine matting the slopes; holds its dark all year.

swatches.name: Azusa river, glacial jade
swatches.note: Snowmelt carrying rock flour; muted jade, never tropical.

swatches.name: North-face rime, blue-white
swatches.note: First frost on shaded rock; the only true near-white, and rare.

swatches.name: Nanakamado rowan, berry red
swatches.note: Mountain-ash berries on the approach trail, the one saturated red.

d.text: Pale granite is the whole cirque; everything else sits on it.

s.text: Larch gold, creeping-pine dark, and glacial jade — the living slope.

a.text: Rime white and rowan red as small, late reads.

palettes.refuses: Snowcap white as primary. In October the Hotaka is rock and gold, not the white pyramid of the brochure.

palettes.kicker: 51° N · July · 08:00 · Moraine Lake, Valley of the Ten Peaks, Canadian Rockies
palettes.title: Early light on the lake when the rock-flour turquoise is at full saturation under limestone walls
palettes.source: Color from the Rockies in high summer: rock-flour glacial turquoise, Cambrian limestone grey, lodgepole and Engelmann spruce, alpine larch not yet turned, and the iron-stained scree of the peaks.

swatches.name: Limestone wall, dove grey
swatches.note: Cambrian sedimentary rock forming the Ten Peaks.

swatches.name: Rock-flour turquoise
swatches.note: Glacial silt suspended in the lake; the signature read, kept honest.

swatches.name: Engelmann spruce, blue-green
swatches.note: Subalpine conifer ringing the shore.

swatches.name: Larch & shore grass green
swatches.note: Summer alpine larch and meadow before the autumn turn.

swatches.name: Iron-stained scree, rust
swatches.note: Oxidised rockfall streaking the lower walls.

swatches.name: Glacier shadow, cold blue
swatches.note: Shaded ice in the high couloirs at this early hour.

d.text: Limestone grey is the mass of the peaks.

s.text: Turquoise water, spruce, and summer green.

a.text: Rust scree and glacier-shadow blue only as accents.

palettes.refuses: Cranked postcard turquoise. The real lake is a soft mineral blue-green; the oversaturated version is a filter, not a place.

palettes.kicker: 43° S · February · 18:00 · Aoraki / Mount Cook, Southern Alps, New Zealand
palettes.title: Late summer evening over the tussock flats with the schist screes catching low gold
palettes.source: Color from the Southern Alps in austral summer: golden snow tussock, grey schist scree, mountain beech, the milky Tasman glacial lake, and the lupin-free braided riverbed of grey gravel.

swatches.name: Snow tussock, dry gold
swatches.note: Tall alpine tussock grass covering the valley flats.

swatches.name: Schist scree, silver grey
swatches.note: Mica-bearing rock that flashes in low light.

swatches.name: Mountain beech, deep green
swatches.note: Southern beech forest on the lower slopes.

swatches.name: Tasman lake, glacial milk
swatches.note: Milky meltwater under the terminal moraine.

swatches.name: Braided gravel, warm stone
swatches.note: Greywacke riverbed shingle of the glacial outwash.

swatches.name: Evening alpenglow, rose
swatches.note: Last light on the summit snow, brief and pink.

d.text: Tussock gold fills the flats from valley to scree line.

s.text: Schist, beech, and glacial milk — the frame around the gold.

a.text: Gravel stone and alpenglow rose as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Lupin-pink riverbeds. The famous lupins are an invasive import; the native braid is grey gravel and gold grass.

palettes.kicker: 51° S · November · 07:00 · Torres del Paine, Patagonian Andes, Chile
palettes.title: Spring dawn on the granite towers with lenga still bare and the calafate just leafing
palettes.source: Color from Patagonia in spring: pink-grey granite towers, the famous milky Lago Pehoé blue, lenga southern-beech bark, dead pale pampas grass, and the deep red of a calafate berry.

swatches.name: Pampas grass, bleached straw
swatches.note: Wind-flattened steppe grass below the massif.

swatches.name: Paine granite, pink-grey
swatches.note: The intrusive granite of the towers, faintly rose.

swatches.name: Lago Pehoé, milky blue
swatches.note: Glacial-flour lake blue under the towers.

swatches.name: Lenga beech bark, grey-brown
swatches.note: Southern beech trunks, still bare in early spring.

swatches.name: Calafate berry, deep violet-red
swatches.note: The fruit that 'brings you back to Patagonia'; rare saturated read.

swatches.name: Caracara wing, slate black
swatches.note: The crested caracara overhead; near-black punctuation.

d.text: Bleached pampas grass is the foreground everywhere.

s.text: Granite, lake blue, and bare lenga — the massif and its water.

a.text: Calafate red and caracara black as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Glacier-blue saturation. Patagonian light is flat and silver more often than not; the electric blue is a calm-day exception.

volumes.title: Boreal & Taiga
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol II · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the northern forest, where conifer meets cold.
preface: The boreal belt circles the globe in a band of spruce, larch, birch, and bog. Its colour is darker and cooler than the deciduous forest to the south, and in winter it reduces almost to graphite and snow.
Each refuses the Christmas-card evergreen: real taiga green is blue-black and sparse, set against muskeg rust and lichen grey.

palettes.kicker: 63° N · February · 13:00 · Karelian taiga, eastern Finland, deep winter
palettes.title: Midday in the frozen forest when the low sun never clears the spruce tops
palettes.source: Color from the Finnish taiga at its coldest: snow-laden kuusi spruce, birch trunks, a frozen lake under snow, beard lichen, and the brief peach of a sun that barely rises.

swatches.name: Snow on spruce, blue-white
swatches.note: Loaded snow on the canopy; the dominant winter surface.

swatches.name: Kuusi spruce, blue-black
swatches.note: Norway spruce showing dark where snow has not settled.

swatches.name: Birch trunk, grey-white
swatches.note: Silver birch bark, the only verticals with light.

swatches.name: Frozen lake, slate
swatches.note: Wind-scoured lake ice under thin snow.

swatches.name: Beard lichen, sage grey-green
swatches.note: Usnea hanging from the spruce, pale and dry.

swatches.name: Low-sun peach, brief warmth
swatches.note: The sun's short arc tints the snow for an hour.

d.text: Snow covers the canopy and floor; it is most of the field.

s.text: Spruce dark, birch trunk, and lake slate inside the white.

a.text: Lichen sage and low-sun peach as brief reads.

palettes.refuses: Christmas-card green. The winter taiga is blue-white and graphite; saturated evergreen belongs to a greeting card.

palettes.kicker: 54° N · September · 16:00 · Larch taiga, Baikal hinterland, Siberia
palettes.title: Early autumn when the larch turns and the whole forest goes briefly amber before the needles drop
palettes.source: Color from Siberian listvennitsa larch forest in turn: gold deciduous-conifer needles, birch yellow, dark taiga spruce behind, peat-brown forest floor, and lingonberry red in the understorey.

swatches.name: Larch needles, autumn amber
swatches.note: Siberian larch — a conifer that turns gold and sheds.

swatches.name: Birch leaf, lemon yellow
swatches.note: Birch turning alongside the larch.

swatches.name: Background spruce, dark teal
swatches.note: Evergreen taiga behind the deciduous burst.

swatches.name: Peat floor, umber
swatches.note: Needle litter and peat underfoot.

swatches.name: Lingonberry, cranberry red
swatches.note: Ground berries in the moss, small and bright.

swatches.name: Taiga sky, cold pale blue
swatches.note: Thin continental autumn sky.

d.text: Larch amber dominates the canopy for two short weeks.

s.text: Birch yellow, spruce teal, and peat brown.

a.text: Lingonberry red and pale sky as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Uniform evergreen. The larch taiga's defining trick is that it turns gold and bare — a conifer that behaves like a birch.

palettes.kicker: 49° N · October · 15:00 · Boreal shield, northern Ontario, Canada
palettes.title: Autumn on the Canadian shield where black spruce stand in muskeg and the tamarack glows
palettes.source: Color from the boreal shield: black spruce spires, orange tamarack larch, rust-red muskeg bog, exposed pink granite of the shield, and the tannin-black water of a beaver pond.

swatches.name: Muskeg bog, rust red
swatches.note: Sphagnum and sedge bog turning in autumn.

swatches.name: Black spruce, near-black green
swatches.note: Spindly bog spruce, the vertical accents of the shield.

swatches.name: Tamarack, burnt orange
swatches.note: Eastern larch glowing before needle drop.

swatches.name: Shield granite, pink-grey
swatches.note: Exposed Precambrian rock between the bogs.

swatches.name: Beaver pond, tannin black
swatches.note: Tea-dark standing water stained by peat.

swatches.name: Lichen rock, mint grey
swatches.note: Map lichen crusting the granite.

d.text: Rust muskeg is the ground across the lowlands.

s.text: Spruce black, tamarack orange, shield granite.

a.text: Tannin water and lichen grey as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Lush forest-green. The shield is sparse, boggy, and rust-toned; the dense green canopy is a more southern forest.

palettes.kicker: 63° N · September · 17:00 · Taiga–tundra edge below Denali, interior Alaska
palettes.title: Fall on the transition zone where aspen gold meets the first red of tundra on the slopes above
palettes.source: Color from the Alaskan interior at treeline: quaking aspen gold, dwarf-birch tundra crimson on the ridges, white spruce, braided glacial river silt, and the deep blue of distant shadowed peaks.

swatches.name: Aspen, quaking gold
swatches.note: Trembling aspen lighting the valley floor.

swatches.name: Tundra dwarf-birch, crimson
swatches.note: Betula nana turning red on the slopes above the trees.

swatches.name: White spruce, grey-green
swatches.note: The dominant interior conifer.

swatches.name: Glacial silt river, pale grey
swatches.note: Braided meltwater carrying rock flour.

swatches.name: Distant peak shadow, blue
swatches.note: The Alaska Range in shade beyond the valley.

swatches.name: Fireweed seed, dusty rose
swatches.note: Gone-over fireweed stalks along the road.

d.text: Aspen gold fills the valley in the last warm week.

s.text: Tundra crimson, spruce, and glacial silt.

a.text: Peak-shadow blue and fireweed rose as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Monolithic green wilderness. The interior in fall is gold and red on grey, with green reduced to the spruce.

volumes.title: Temperate Rainforest
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol III · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the wet forest, where everything is green on green.
preface: Temperate rainforest is the densest green on earth — moss on bark on fern on nurse log — but read closely it is a dozen greens plus the brown of decay and the tannin-black of its streams. The challenge is to find the structure inside the green.
Each refuses the flat 'jungle green': the real forest separates into moss, conifer, fern, and rot, each a distinct hue.

palettes.kicker: 47° N · April · 10:00 · Hoh Rain Forest, Olympic Peninsula, Washington
palettes.title: Spring under the bigleaf maples when every branch carries a fur of club moss
palettes.source: Color from the Hoh: club moss draping the maples, Sitka spruce dark, sword-fern green, a red-brown nurse log, and the grey light filtering through layers of canopy.

swatches.name: Club moss, electric green
swatches.note: Selaginella draping every maple branch; the dominant glow.

swatches.name: Sitka spruce, deep green
swatches.note: The towering conifer behind the moss.

swatches.name: Sword fern, mid green
swatches.note: Dense fern understorey across the floor.

swatches.name: Nurse log, red-brown decay
swatches.note: A fallen trunk feeding the next generation.

swatches.name: Filtered light, grey-green
swatches.note: Diffuse canopy light, the forest's ambient tone.

swatches.name: Banana slug, ochre yellow
swatches.note: The forest's famous decomposer on the trail.

d.text: Club-moss green coats the canopy and dominates the eye.

s.text: Spruce, fern, and nurse-log brown give the green its structure.

a.text: Filtered light and slug ochre as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Flat jungle green. The Hoh is a layered system of distinct greens; one green flattens it into a stock photo.

palettes.kicker: 52° N · September · 09:00 · Great Bear Rainforest, coastal British Columbia
palettes.title: A salmon stream in early autumn with cedar overhead and the first spawners arriving
palettes.source: Color from the Great Bear coast: western red cedar bark, deep hemlock green, a tannin-stained stream, the silver-pink of a spawning salmon, and the cream coat of a rare spirit bear.

swatches.name: Hemlock canopy, dark green
swatches.note: Western hemlock shading the whole valley.

swatches.name: Red cedar bark, cinnamon
swatches.note: Shredding cedar bark, warm against the green.

swatches.name: Tannin stream, amber black
swatches.note: Tea-coloured creek carrying forest tannins.

swatches.name: Spawning salmon, dusty rose
swatches.note: Chum and pink salmon flushing red to spawn.

swatches.name: Spirit bear, cream
swatches.note: The rare white-phase black bear fishing the stream.

swatches.name: Skunk cabbage, acid yellow
swatches.note: Bog-edge spathe, the brightest understorey note.

d.text: Hemlock green is the canopy mass of the inlet.

s.text: Cedar cinnamon, tannin water, and salmon rose.

a.text: Spirit-bear cream and skunk-cabbage yellow as rare reads.

palettes.refuses: Tropical-reef colour. This is a cold green coast — the brightness is salmon and lichen, not coral.

palettes.kicker: 41° S · May · 11:00 · Tarkine / takayna, northwest Tasmania
palettes.title: Autumn in the cool temperate rainforest where myrtle beech turns and the rivers run the colour of tea
palettes.source: Color from the Tarkine: myrtle beech canopy, button-grass plain gold, sassafras green, the famous tannin-brown Pieman river, and a splash of native fagus turning rust.

swatches.name: Myrtle beech, forest green
swatches.note: Nothofagus canopy of the cool rainforest.

swatches.name: Button-grass plain, tawny gold
swatches.note: Open moorland sedge between the forest patches.

swatches.name: Tannin river, root-beer brown
swatches.note: The Pieman, stained dark by buttongrass tannins.

swatches.name: Sassafras leaf, bright green
swatches.note: Understorey sassafras catching gaps of light.

swatches.name: Fagus, deciduous rust
swatches.note: Tasmania's only winter-deciduous tree, the 'turning of the fagus'.

swatches.name: Man-fern frond, dark olive
swatches.note: Ancient tree-fern fronds in the gullies.

d.text: Myrtle-beech green is the closed canopy.

s.text: Button-grass gold, tannin river, and sassafras green.

a.text: Fagus rust and tree-fern olive as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Eucalypt blue-grey. The Tarkine is rainforest, not dry sclerophyll; its base note is wet green, not silver-blue.

palettes.kicker: 40° S · December · 14:00 · Valdivian rainforest, Los Ríos, southern Chile
palettes.title: Summer beneath the ancient alerce with nalca rhubarb spreading giant leaves over the stream
palettes.source: Color from the Valdivian forest: reddish alerce conifer bark, giant nalca leaf green, copihue-flower crimson, volcanic black soil, and the pale blue of a glacial río beyond the trees.

swatches.name: Nalca leaf, rich green
swatches.note: Chilean rhubarb's huge leaves carpeting the stream banks.

swatches.name: Alerce bark, red-brown
swatches.note: Bark of the millennia-old Patagonian cypress.

swatches.name: Volcanic soil, near-black
swatches.note: Dark andisol underfoot from regional eruptions.

swatches.name: Fern & canopy, deep green
swatches.note: The layered evergreen canopy overhead.

swatches.name: Copihue flower, crimson
swatches.note: Chile's national flower, a bell-red vine bloom.

swatches.name: Glacial río, pale blue
swatches.note: Andean meltwater river glimpsed past the trees.

d.text: Nalca green dominates every clearing and bank.

s.text: Alerce bark, black soil, and canopy green.

a.text: Copihue crimson and glacial blue as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Tropical saturation. Valdivian forest is a cool, dark, ancient green — the brights are a single flower, not the whole scene.

volumes.title: Tundra & Polar
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol IV · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from beyond the trees, where ground-cover replaces forest.
preface: Above and beyond the treeline the world flattens to lichen, dwarf shrub, rock, and ice. The colour is low and wide — but in autumn the tundra briefly burns red and gold across whole hillsides.
Each refuses the all-white polar cliché: the Arctic is rust, ochre, and ground-hugging crimson far more of the year than it is snow.

palettes.kicker: 70° N · September · 12:00 · Varanger / Finnmark tundra, Arctic Norway
palettes.title: Autumn on the open fjell when the dwarf birch turns the whole hillside crimson
palettes.source: Color from the Finnmark tundra in turn: dwarf birch crimson, reindeer-lichen grey, bilberry red, weathered gneiss, and the steel of the Barents Sea.

swatches.name: Dwarf birch, tundra crimson
swatches.note: Betula nana reddening across the open fjell.

swatches.name: Reindeer lichen, pale grey
swatches.note: Cladonia mats covering the ground between shrubs.

swatches.name: Bilberry leaf, wine red
swatches.note: Ground bilberry turning deep red in the cold.

swatches.name: Gneiss boulder, blue-grey
swatches.note: Glacier-dropped bedrock erratics.

swatches.name: Barents Sea, steel blue
swatches.note: The cold Arctic sea on the horizon.

swatches.name: Cloudberry, amber gold
swatches.note: Ripe cloudberries in the bog, prized and small.

d.text: Dwarf-birch crimson covers the hillsides in early autumn.

s.text: Lichen grey, bilberry wine, and gneiss blue-grey.

a.text: Sea steel and cloudberry gold as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Snow-white Arctic. For weeks each autumn the tundra is the reddest landscape in Europe, not a white one.

palettes.kicker: 78° N · July · 14:00 · Spitsbergen interior, Svalbard
palettes.title: High-Arctic summer where saxifrage flecks the scree below a calving glacier
palettes.source: Color from Svalbard in polar summer: grey frost-shattered scree, glacier blue-white, purple saxifrage, tundra-moss green, and the rust of iron-stained rock.

swatches.name: Frost-shattered scree, cold grey
swatches.note: Endless angular rock of the polar desert.

swatches.name: Glacier front, blue-white
swatches.note: Calving ice cliff above the valley.

swatches.name: Tundra moss, muted green
swatches.note: Thin moss-and-sedge cover in sheltered hollows.

swatches.name: Iron-stained rock, ochre rust
swatches.note: Oxidised mineral streaks on the slopes.

swatches.name: Purple saxifrage, magenta
swatches.note: The first Arctic bloom, low against the rock.

swatches.name: Meltwater pool, pale glacial
swatches.note: Silt-milky melt collecting on the flats.

d.text: Grey scree is the polar desert's overwhelming surface.

s.text: Glacier blue-white, moss green, and iron rust.

a.text: Saxifrage magenta and meltwater blue as rare reads.

palettes.refuses: Uniform white ice-cap. Most of summer Svalbard is grey rock and rust, with ice as the backdrop, not the field.

palettes.kicker: 66° N · August · 16:00 · Baffin Island fjord, Nunavut, Canadian Arctic
palettes.title: Late summer on the tundra above a fjord where cottongrass nods over lichen rock
palettes.source: Color from Baffin in brief summer: cottongrass white tufts, orange map-lichen rock, Arctic-willow green, fjord blue-black, and the pink of river-beauty fireweed.

swatches.name: Tundra sedge, olive green
swatches.note: Low sedge-and-grass mat of the summer tundra.

swatches.name: Map lichen, orange crust

swatches.name: Cottongrass, tuft white
swatches.note: Eriophorum seedheads bobbing over the bog.

swatches.name: Fjord water, blue-black
swatches.note: Deep cold fjord below the tundra bench.

swatches.name: River-beauty fireweed, pink
swatches.note: Dwarf fireweed along the meltwater channels.

swatches.name: Gabbro rock, charcoal
swatches.note: Dark intrusive bedrock of the highlands.

d.text: Olive sedge is the tundra carpet over the bench.

s.text: Lichen orange, cottongrass white, and fjord blue-black.

a.text: Fireweed pink and gabbro charcoal as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Polar-bear-on-white iconography. The summer Arctic is green, orange, and blue-black ground, not a white sheet.

palettes.kicker: 64° S · January · 18:00 · Antarctic Peninsula, austral summer evening
palettes.title: Summer at a gentoo colony where pink krill-guano stains the snow below black basalt
palettes.source: Color from the Peninsula: blue glacier ice, black volcanic basalt, the pink-orange of krill guano staining a penguin colony, snow-algae green, and the steel of the Southern Ocean.

swatches.name: Glacier ice, pale blue
swatches.note: Compressed glacial ice, blue where it's dense.

swatches.name: Volcanic basalt, black

swatches.name: Southern Ocean, steel grey
swatches.note: The cold sea between the bergs.

swatches.name: Snow algae, faint green
swatches.note: Green snow-algae blooming on summer drifts.

swatches.name: Krill guano, salmon pink

swatches.name: Gentoo beak, coral orange

s.text: Basalt black, ocean steel, and faint snow-algae green.

a.text: Krill-guano pink and gentoo orange as small reads.

volumes.title: Desert & Arid
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol V · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the dry lands, where the rock outshouts the plants.
preface: Deserts are mineral landscapes: the dominant colour is almost always the ground itself — iron-red sand, salt-white pan, copper rock — with sparse vegetation reduced to silver and grey. Light is hard and shadows are sharp.
Each refuses the empty-golden-dune cliché: real deserts are specific reds, whites, and ochres set by the local mineralogy, with one or two living greens.

palettes.kicker: 24° S · June · 07:00 · Sossusvlei, Namib Desert, Namibia
palettes.title: Dawn on the red dunes with dead camelthorn black on the white clay pan of Deadvlei
palettes.source: Color from the Namib at sunrise: iron-oxide red dune, the white cracked clay of Deadvlei, black 900-year-old camelthorn skeletons, the shaded blue-violet of the dune's lee, and coastal fog grey.

swatches.name: Iron-oxide dune, brick red
swatches.note: Ancient wind-sorted sand, reddened by iron coating.

swatches.name: Deadvlei pan, cracked white
swatches.note: Sun-baked clay floor of the dead marsh.

swatches.name: Camelthorn skeleton, charcoal black
swatches.note: Trees dead 900 years, too dry to rot.

swatches.name: Dune lee, shadow violet
swatches.note: The shaded face of the dune before the sun clears it.

swatches.name: Coastal fog, pale grey
swatches.note: Atlantic fog that creeps inland at dawn.

swatches.name: Welwitschia, dust green
swatches.note: The desert's ancient two-leaved survivor plant.

d.text: Red dune sand is the overwhelming surface and horizon.

s.text: White pan, black trees, and violet shadow give it depth.

a.text: Fog grey and welwitschia green as small reads.

palettes.refuses: Golden-hour generic dune. The Namib's signature is brick-red sand against white clay and black trees — a hard tricolour, not a soft glow.

palettes.kicker: 37° N · October · 16:00 · Monument Valley, Colorado Plateau, Arizona–Utah
palettes.title: Late afternoon when the sandstone buttes go orange-red over blue sage and dark juniper
palettes.source: Color from the Plateau: Wingate sandstone red, blue-green sagebrush, dark Utah juniper, pale alkali wash, and the deep blue of the high-desert sky behind the buttes.

swatches.name: Wingate sandstone, orange-red
swatches.note: Iron-rich cliff sandstone of the buttes.

swatches.name: Big sagebrush, blue-grey green

swatches.name: Utah juniper, dark olive
swatches.note: Scattered junipers, the tallest green.

swatches.name: Alkali wash, pale buff
swatches.note: Dry sandy washes between the rock.

swatches.name: High-desert sky, deep blue

swatches.name: Indian paintbrush, scarlet

volumes.title: Tropical Rainforest
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol VI · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the hot forest, where the brights live in flashes.
preface: Tropical rainforest reads as a wall of green, but its identity is set by the flashes that punctuate it — a macaw, a poison frog, a heliconia — against muddy water and leaf-litter brown. The structure is dark green base, loud rare accent.
Each refuses the candy-bright jungle: the forest is mostly shadow and mud, and the colour earns its loudness by being rare.

volumes.title: Wetland, Bog & Delta
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol VII · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from where land and water blur, reed by reed.
preface: Wetlands are horizontal worlds of reed, water, and sky, their colour set by the plant that dominates the marsh and the way light lies flat across open water. They shift hard between seasons — green flood to gold dry.
Each refuses the murky-swamp cliché: wetlands are luminous, with sky-water and reed-gold doing most of the work.

volumes.title: Grassland, Steppe & Savanna
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol VIII · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the open ground, all grass and sky and dust.
preface: Grasslands are dominated by two huge fields — the grass and the sky — with everything else a small punctuation: a tree, an animal, a flower. The grass colour is set by the season: green flush, gold cure, grey dormancy.
Each refuses the lush-green-meadow default: most of the year, grassland is cured gold and dust, not spring green.

volumes.title: Coast, Reef & Littoral
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol IX · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the tide line, where water meets the living rock.
preface: The coast is an edge biome, its colour split between the water's blue-green and the dark, encrusted intertidal zone. Each shore is set by its dominant organism — coral, rockweed, mangrove, kelp — and the temperature of its sea.
Each refuses the brochure-beach turquoise: most productive coasts are cold, dark, and weed-covered, with brightness coming from the life, not the water.

volumes.title: Volcanic & Geothermal
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol X · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the restless ground, where chemistry colours the earth.
preface: Volcanic country is coloured by mineralogy and microbiology: sulphur yellow, iron red, obsidian black, and the impossible blues and oranges of thermophile-rich pools. The base is dark rock; the accents are chemical and intense.
Each refuses the generic lava-orange: real geothermal colour is the mineral stain and the microbial mat, far stranger than a glow.

volumes.title: Cave, Karst & Subterranean
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol XI · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from below the surface, lit by a single shaft.
preface: Underground, colour comes from mineral deposition and the quality of the one light source — a shaft, a lamp, a glow. The base is near-black; everything is read against the dark, which makes even pale stone glow.
Each refuses the flat-black 'cave' cliché: subterranean palettes are about luminous mineral against shadow, not an absence of colour.

volumes.title: Deciduous & Temperate Woodland
volumes.eyebrow: Biome · Vol XII · Sourced Palette Study
volumes.h1: Four palettes from the broadleaf wood, tuned to a single season.
preface: Deciduous woodland is the great colour-changer: the same forest is bluebell-blue in spring, deep green in summer, and a riot of red and gold in autumn. Each palette fixes one moment in that cycle.
Each refuses the generic autumn-leaf medley: a real wood at a real moment is dominated by one or two species' colour, not an even confetti of all of them.

palettes.source: Color from New England at peak: sugar-maple scarlet, maple orange, birch-and-aspen yellow, white birch trunk, and the dark green of a remnant hemlock.
